1:
The song opens with a heading the witnesses read as both a date and a summary of all that follows. Matthew Henry draws four lessons from it in order. First, that it has often been the lot of God's people to have many enemies and to stand in imminent danger of falling into their hands; David was a man after God's own heart but not after men's heart, and Saul is singled out either as distinct from the heathen foes — for David would not reckon among his enemies the king he himself refused to hate — or, more likely, as the chief and most malicious of them: 'Let not those whom God loves marvel if the world hate them.' Second, that those who trust God in the way of duty find him a present help in their greatest dangers, and that remarkable preservations should be mentioned in our praises with particular emphasis. Third, that those who receive signal mercies owe him the glory of them, for 'every new mercy in our hand should put a new song into our mouth.' Fourth, that thankful returns should be speedy: in the very day of deliverance David sang, while the mercy was fresh and the devout affections still warm, that the thank-offering might be kindled with the fire of them.

The grammatical-historical commentators fix the setting and shape. Keil and Delitzsch place the psalm in the later years of David's reign, when the LORD had rescued him from all his foes, and map its architecture: an introduction (vv.2–4), a first half picturing deliverance under the image of a great theophany (vv.5–20) with its ground in David's integrity (vv.21–28), a second half of victory over the nations (vv.29–46), and a closing strophe of praise (vv.47–51) — two leading divisions framed by an opening and a concluding strophe, though without any rigid system of strophes between. John Gill, characteristically, refers the exposition to its twin, for the song stands again, nearly word for word, as Psalm 18.

That very likeness raises the debate that runs through the chapter. Adam Clarke heads the psalm a thanksgiving that carries 'prophetic declarations relative to the humiliation and exaltation of the Messiah,' and reserves the full exposition for the Psalter. Henry keeps the historical David in view but reads him as a type, so that 'all his joys and hopes close, as all our hopes should do, in the great Redeemer.' The witnesses thus agree that the song is David's grateful record of past deliverance, and already begin to divide over how far its language reaches beyond David to great David's greater Son — a question v.11 will press hardest.
2:
The introduction proper begins, and the witnesses gather round the piled-up names David gives his God: rock, fortress, deliverer. Keil and Delitzsch observe that vv.2–4 contain the sum and substance of the whole psalm: David groups the many deliverances of his agitated life into one long series of predicates in which he extols God as his defence and refuge. The heaping up is deliberate, an expression at once of liveliest gratitude for the past and of settled hope for the future; and the titles, they insist, are not vocatives set in apposition to Jehovah but declarations about him — how he had proved himself faithful in every calamity and would prove so still. They note that David calls God 'my rock and my castle' in Psalm 31 as well, so that the language is his tried and habitual confession.

The puritan Matthew Henry turns the same predicates into worship, and reads them as the foundation of the whole song. He hears David first adoring God's incomparable perfections — 'Who is God, save the LORD? ... Who is a rock, save our God?' (v.32) — the idols being dead while the LORD liveth (v.47); then triumphing in his interest in this God, which he lays as the ground of every benefit: 'He is my God; and, if my God, then my rock.' Whatever is David's strength and support, it is the God of his rock that makes it so. The fortress is the high tower in which he is out of reach of real evils, the tower of salvation (v.51) that can never be battered or undermined; and the horn of salvation by which he is protected and his enemies pushed down — a title, Henry adds, given to Christ in the house of David (Luke 1:69). Am I in distress? he is my deliverer; struck at, shot at? my shield; pursued? my refuge; oppressed? my saviour.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott adds a textual note that will recur through the chapter: this recension lacks the opening line of the Psalm, 'I will love thee, O LORD, my strength' (Psalm 18:1), a line that there forms a fitting overture to the whole. John Gill, reformed and brief, again sends the reader to his fuller exposition on the parallel. The voices do not divide here; with one accord they take these opening titles as David's confession that his God is himself his safety — the security that will be unfolded, danger by danger, in all that follows.
3:
The catalogue of titles continues — the God of my rock, shield, horn of salvation, high tower, refuge, saviour — and the witnesses attend both to the trust these names invite and to the textual peculiarities of this recension. Charles Ellicott notes that where the Psalm reads 'My God, my rock,' the two expressions are here fused into one, 'The God of my rock,' and that the recurrence of a similar phrase in v.47 — a phrase absent from the Psalm — shows the fusion was intentional and not a copyist's slip. He observes too that the closing words, 'and my refuge, my saviour; thou savest me from violence,' are wanting in the Psalm, being there compensated in part by the fuller opening line. Keil and Delitzsch keep the same predicates in view as the continuation of the introductory strophe, in which David piles title upon title to declare how God had approved himself his defence in all the calamities of his life, and would still.

The reformed John Gill, brief as ever, takes the clause 'in him will I trust' as the hinge of the whole confession and refers it to his exposition of the twin passage; the naming of God is not bare description but the ground of reliance. This chimes with Matthew Henry's reading at v.2, that the first improvement David makes of his interest in God is resolve — 'In him will I trust,' that is, to resign himself to God's direction and then depend on his power, wisdom, and goodness to conduct him well.

Beneath the small textual differences the witnesses find a single, steadying confession. Every title has its answering peril: shield against the shot, horn to push down the foe, high tower above the reach of evil, refuge for the pursued, saviour of the oppressed — all gathered into the last words, 'thou savest me from violence.' David does not merely believe that God can deliver; he stakes his soul on it, and the whole song is the vindication of that trust. The care with which the later recension weighs and even doubles these names — as the closing survey of the chapter will show — testifies that the singer and his copyists alike counted every syllable of this confession precious, refusing to let one of God's names for himself fall to the ground.
4:
The introductory strophe closes with a resolve and a certainty: 'I will call on the LORD, who is worthy to be praised: so shall I be saved from mine enemies.' Keil and Delitzsch render it, 'I call upon the praised one, Jehovah, and I am saved from my enemies,' and read it as the settled confidence in which David sums up his whole experience — prayer directed to the God who deserves all praise issues, as a matter of course, in deliverance. The calling and the saving are bound together as cause and effect; the God whose titles have just been rehearsed is precisely the God who answers, so that the verse is less a request than a rule of David's life proved a thousand times over.

The puritan Matthew Henry, expounding the whole song at v.2, makes this the second improvement David draws from his interest in God: 'On him I will call, for he is worthy to be praised.' What we have found in God that deserves praise ought to draw us to pray to him and to give him glory; adoration and petition rise together, and neither is complete without the other. The reformed John Gill refers the reader again to his fuller notes, content to let the verse stand as the pledge that answers the opening titles.

Though the material here is spare, its logic is the spine of the psalm, and the witnesses read it alike. The verse looks two ways at once. Backward, it gathers up the confession of vv.2–3: because God is rock, fortress, shield, and horn of salvation, he is 'worthy to be praised.' Forward, it announces the theme the rest of the song will prove: 'so shall I be saved from mine enemies.' Everything that follows — the terrors of death in vv.5–6, the cry from the depths in v.7, the descending God of vv.8–16, and the rescue of vv.17–20 — is the demonstration of this quiet certainty. David states the outcome before he narrates the danger, because he sings not as one hoping but as one who has already been saved, and would have every reader learn to call on the same God with the same expectation.
5:
David turns from confidence to the peril it overcame, and the imagery grows dark: 'When the waves of death compassed me, the floods of ungodly men made me afraid.' Keil and Delitzsch prefer the reading 'breakers of death' to the Psalm's 'snares of death,' arguing that the parallelism with 'streams of Belial' commends the watery figure: David's distress is pictured as violent floods breaking one upon another, so that the waters close over a man who cannot help himself. They gather into these verses not only the persecutions under Saul but every mortal danger of his life — the wars, the giant of 2 Samuel 21:16, the conspiracy of Absalom — as the many perils out of which the one Lord delivered him.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott weighs the same variant more coolly. Where the Psalm has literally 'the bands of death,' this recension has an entirely different word, and 'the variation can hardly have been accidental,' the form here answering better to the parallelism of the next clause. Such small, deliberate differences, he holds, mark the two texts as distinct recensions rather than careless copies. The reformed John Gill refers the picture to his exposition of the parallel, taking the waves and floods as the overwhelming assaults of ungodly men against the persecuted saint.

Here the chapter's great debate first shows its head. Adam Clarke grants that in a primary sense many of these things belong to David, 'yet generally and fully they belong to the Messiah alone' — the waves and cords of death pointing beyond David's escapes to a greater deliverance out of death itself. Matthew Henry, expounding the danger at v.2, keeps David in the foreground: he was surrounded with death on every side, threatened to be overwhelmed and seeing no way of escape, the floods of Belial — 'the wicked one, and his wicked instruments' — making him afraid, so that he trembled to see not earth only, but death and hell, in arms against him. The witnesses agree on the terror of the scene; they begin to differ, as Clarke signals, over whose extremity it finally describes — the hunted David, or the suffering Christ whom David foreshadows.
6:
The flood-imagery deepens into the language of the grave: 'The sorrows of hell compassed me about; the snares of death prevented me.' The word rendered 'sorrows' is, in the parallel tradition, the cords or bands that bind, so that David is pictured as one already netted and hauled toward Sheol, the snares of death sprung about him before he could escape. Keil and Delitzsch keep these verses together as the picture of a man engulfed — the breakers of death, the streams of Belial, the cords of hell, the snares of death — the whole abyss opening under him, and mark that the strophe moves at once from this extremity to the cry of v.7, since only God can reach into such a depth.

The reformed John Gill refers the imagery to his fuller exposition, reading it of the encompassing dangers and the terrors of death that beset the persecuted saint. Matthew Henry, treating the danger at v.2, presses the helplessness: so strongly did the cords and snares of death hold David that he could no more deliver himself than a man in the grave can climb out of it — which is precisely why the next verse turns not to effort but to prayer. The verse thus completes the description of a peril from which there is no natural escape.

The matter is spare in comment but weighty, and it is one on which Adam Clarke's Messianic reading gathers force: sorrows of hell and snares of death that hold a man fast are, on his view, more fully answered in the Passion than in any escape of David's. Yet the witnesses do not quarrel over the sense of the picture itself; all take it as the extremity from which deliverance is impossible to man. What David describes is not mere difficulty but death's own grip — and by placing his cry at the very lip of the grave, he makes the rescue that follows the more plainly God's work and not his own. The trembling of earth and heaven in v.8 is heaven's answer to a man already counted among the dead, and it is the measure of how far the arm of God will reach for one who calls.
7:
Out of the depths comes the cry, and with it the turning-point of the whole song: 'In my distress I called upon the LORD, and cried to my God: and he did hear my voice out of his temple, and my cry did enter into his ears.' Charles Ellicott attends closely to the wording. Here the two verbs 'called' and 'cried' are the same in the original, though they differ in the parallel place in the Psalm; and the closing phrase, literally 'my cry in his ears,' is elliptical, filled out in the Psalm as 'my cry came before him, even into his ears.' The distress and the calling belong together; the very extremity of vv.5–6 is what drives the prayer, so that the darkness of the peril becomes the occasion of the mercy.

The reformed John Gill lets the verse rest on its plain sense, the cry of the afflicted heard in heaven, referring the fuller treatment to the parallel. Matthew Henry, expounding it at v.2, makes it the model of importunate prayer: David 'has here left us a good example, when we are in distress, to cry unto God with importunity, as children in a fright cry to their parents,' and great encouragement to do so, for he found God ready to answer 'out of his temple in heaven, where he is continually served and adored.' The deliverance, Henry adds, was thus an answer to prayer, not a stroke of fortune.

Though few voices speak, the verse is the psalm's pivot, and they agree on its force. All the terror of the preceding lines resolves into a single act — a man at the end of himself lifting his voice — and all the majesty of the lines that follow is God's response to it. The temple from which God hears is not the earthly sanctuary, which David never lived to build, but his heavenly dwelling; the cry that seemed lost in the roar of the floods finds, in fact, its way straight into the ears of the living God. It is because this prayer was heard that the heavens can bow in v.10, and that the singer can look back on the whole ordeal as deliverance rather than defeat. Between the distress of one verse and the earthquake of the next stands nothing but a prayer — and that, the witnesses would have us learn, is enough.
8:
The answer to David's cry breaks out as a storm of theophany: 'Then the earth shook and trembled; the foundations of heaven moved and shook, because he was wroth.' Keil and Delitzsch read the descent that begins here as modelled on Sinai — Jehovah comes down to save his servant as he once came down upon the mount to make his covenant with Israel, amid the terrible natural phenomena that proclaim the wrath of the Almighty, and they hear the same note struck earlier in the Song of Deborah. They weigh the recension's variants: 'foundations of the heavens' rather than 'of the hills,' understood not as pillars on which the sky rests, like so many Atlases, but as the central points of the heavens themselves, so that the whole universe trembles at once.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott marks the same difference. Where this text says 'foundations of heaven,' the Psalm says 'of the hills'; the thought is one, but the strong poetic figure that calls the mountains the pillars of heaven (compare Job 26:11) is softened in the Psalm's wording. The reformed John Gill refers the convulsion to his exposition of the parallel, reading the shaking as the token of God's anger against the enemies of his servant.

The puritan Matthew Henry, expounding these lofty lines at v.2, guards them against a wooden literalism. We do not read that in any of David's battles God fought for him with thunder as in Samuel's day (1 Samuel 7:10), or with hail as in Joshua's, or with the stars in their courses as in Deborah's; these Sinai-metaphors are borrowed to set forth four things — the glory of God manifested in the deliverance, his displeasure against David's enemies, the confusion into which they were thrown 'as if the earth had trembled and the foundations of the world had been discovered,' and how ready he was to help. God's wisdom, power, goodness, and justice appearing for David were as bright a discovery of his glory to the eye of faith as such miracles would have been to the eye of sense. So the quaking earth is the language of faith, not of the barometer: it declares that the God who heard the cry of v.7 is a God before whose anger no creature can stand. The witnesses concur — the trembling world is the measure of the wrath that armed itself for David, and against his foes.
9:
The storm gathers about the person of God himself: 'There went up a smoke out of his nostrils, and fire out of his mouth devoured: coals were kindled by it.' Keil and Delitzsch keep vv.9–11 together as an expansion of the descent, the smoke and devouring fire and kindled coals picturing the burning anger of the God who bows the heavens and comes down. The images are drawn from the Sinai theophany, where the mount smoked and the LORD descended in fire, and they prepare for the lightning that in v.13 bursts from the brightness before him. The bold anthropomorphism — anger figured as smoke from the nostrils and consuming fire from the mouth — belongs to the same ancient poetry that pictures the divine breath and voice as forces of nature.

The reformed John Gill refers the burning imagery to his exposition of the twin psalm, reading it of the anger of God against the enemies of his servant. Matthew Henry, expounding the theophany at v.2, names this verse as the chief instance of the second thing the metaphors set forth — God's displeasure against his foes. Because God so espoused David's cause, he showed himself an enemy to all his enemies; his anger is figured 'by a smoke out of his nostrils, and fire out of his mouth,' with the coals kindled here and the arrows of v.15; and Henry asks, 'Who knows the power and terror of his wrath?'

The verse admits little dispute, and the witnesses do not attempt any; its force lies in its boldness. God is not described as sending an agent, but as breathing out the fire himself — the figure is meant to make the wrath personal and immediate, no cool decree but a burning against evil. For the man drowning in the floods of v.5, there could be no greater comfort than to know that his cause had become God's own quarrel, and that the same LORD who is his rock has turned the heat of his displeasure against those who sought his ruin. The smoke and fire that terrify the enemy are, to the servant, the very tokens of a covenant-love that will not let him perish — the same wrath being ruin to the one and rescue to the other.
10:
'He bowed the heavens also, and came down; and darkness was under his feet.' The single witness recorded here is the reformed John Gill, who refers the line, as throughout the chapter, to his exposition of the parallel psalm; yet the verse itself and its setting yield a full sense. The picture is of the sky itself sinking as the enthroned God stoops toward the earth, the storm-cloud beneath his tread turned to a footstool of thick darkness. It continues the descent begun in v.8 and expanded in v.9: the God whose anger smokes and burns now leaves his height and draws near to the drowning man of v.5. That the heavens are said to bow, and not the man to climb, is the whole weight of the line.

Matthew Henry's exposition of the theophany at v.2 supplies the sense the other commentators leave implicit. These lofty lines are meant, among other things, to show 'how ready God was to help him': though the LORD had seemed at a distance, he hastened to David's succour and came with seasonable relief. Yet he came as 'a God that hideth himself' (Isaiah 45:15); the darkness under his feet belongs with the pavilion of cloud in v.12, for God 'made darkness his pavilion' — a covering both for the amazement of his enemies and for the protection of his own people.

The bowing of the heavens is thus the great condescension of the psalm, and the witnesses, few as they are, read it so. The immeasurable God does not summon David up to himself; he bends the very sky and comes down. The darkness that shrouds his approach is not distance but nearness veiled — the same cloud that blinds the foe hides and shelters the friend. What began as a cry lost in the floods has become the descent of the Almighty, and the reader is meant to feel the awe of it: that the trembling of earth in v.8 is God himself moving toward one afflicted man because he heard him call. Heaven stoops to earth before earth is ever asked to rise.
11:
'And he rode upon a cherub, and did fly: and he was seen upon the wings of the wind.' This verse draws the chapter's fullest comment, and brings its great debate to a head. Adam Clarke dwells first on the poetry, setting out the Hebrew and marvelling how 'the clap of the wing, the agitation and rush through the air' are matched in the very sound of the words. Then he appends Dr. Kennicott's long dissertation, which turns the whole song into a controversy over its speaker. Kennicott argues that the sublime description — the sufferer whose agonies convulse nature, who then takes vengeance on his own people and becomes head of the heathen — cannot fit David; that if it were of David only it would be no prophecy at all, but mere thanksgiving for favours past; and that since Paul quotes it of the conversion of the Gentiles (Romans 15:9; Hebrews 2:13), the song 'relates only to the Messiah.' The title, on this view, marks merely the time when David composed the prophecy, and Josephus is cited that both this ode and the 'last words' of the next chapter (2 Samuel 23) were written when his wars were over.

The grammatical-historical voices resist so exclusive a reading. Keil and Delitzsch explain the figure historically: the cherub is no earthly creature but the angelic throne-bearer, and 'riding upon the cherub' is borrowed from God's enthronement between the cherubim above the ark of the covenant (Exodus 25:20; compare 2 Samuel 6:2). Charles Ellicott notes that the recension's 'he was seen' stands where the Psalm has the more poetical 'he did fly,' the two Hebrew words being exceedingly alike and easily confused. John Gill refers the flight to his exposition of the parallel.

So the division is plainest here. Clarke and Kennicott read the song as spoken by the Messiah alone, David excluded even from a double sense, since 'nothing can be intended... to relate to two persons unless it be true of both.' The grammatical-historical commentators keep David the singer, describing his own deliverance in Sinai's borrowed splendour. Between them stands Matthew Henry's typology, which reads David's rescue as real history that yet foreshadows the Christ 'whom God upheld because he delighted in him' (Isaiah 42:1). The cherub-flight itself all agree to be the swift, sovereign descent of God to save; the witnesses part only over whose salvation it finally sings — David's, the Messiah's, or David's as the shadow of his greater Son.
12:
'And he made darkness pavilions round about him, dark waters, and thick clouds of the skies.' Keil and Delitzsch treat the verse as one of the storm-cluster, where the splendour of the divine nature wraps itself in cloud; they linger on the recension's rare word, taking it as a 'massive gathering together' of water — God as the cloud-gatherer, heaping the thick clouds about himself as a tent. They record, but do not finally adopt, the old Jewish reading that likens the clouds to a sieve, straining the rain in close drops upon the earth; their own preference keeps the sense of a dense pavilion of darkness drawn round the advancing God.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott marks a gap in this text: the Psalm reads more fully, 'He made darkness his secret place; his pavilion round about him were dark waters,' and a word appears to have dropped out here, the margin's 'binding' or 'gathering of waters' being the more exact rendering, differing from the Psalm by a single letter. The reformed John Gill refers the pavilion of darkness to his exposition of the parallel, reading the dark waters and thick clouds as the veil of the divine majesty.

The witnesses do not contend over the picture, and Matthew Henry's overview at v.2 supplies its theological point: God 'made darkness his pavilion,' both for the amazement of his enemies and for the protection of his own people, being in this a God that hides himself. The same cloud serves a double office. To the foe it is impenetrable gloom, the terror of a hidden and advancing majesty; to David it is shelter, the curtained tent within which the King who came down to save him cannot be reached by those who hate him. So even the darkness of the theophany is mercy in disguise: the God who veils himself in storm does so not to withdraw but to work, and the thick clouds that frighten the wicked are the very walls of a pavilion within which the afflicted are kept safe. What looks from without like the gathering of a tempest is, from within, the pitching of a tent for the rescued.
13:
'Through the brightness before him were coals of fire kindled.' Keil and Delitzsch read the line as the storm's climax: the splendour of the divine nature, veiled in the clouds of v.12, breaks through its dark covering in burning coals of fire — that is, in the lightning that shoots from the storm-clouds in streams of fire. They note that this recension is 'terribly mutilated' here, only the four letters answering to 'burned' surviving of a fuller line, and that the description passes at once into the thunder and arrows of vv.14–16. The coals are no dying embers but the very fire of heaven loosed against the foe.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott sharpens the rendering: not 'through' but 'out of the brightness,' and he judges the Psalm's fuller form the more exact representation of the original — 'Out of the brightness before him his thick clouds passed, hail stones and coals of fire.' The recension has here contracted what the Psalm preserves, another of the small differences that mark the two as distinct texts of one great hymn. The reformed John Gill again refers the imagery to his exposition of the twin, reading the kindled coals of the fire of God's wrath against his enemies.

The verse is brief and its text uncertain, but its place in the theophany is clear, and the witnesses agree upon it. The God who wrapped himself in cloud does not stay hidden; the brightness of his presence tears the darkness with fire. The lightning is at once weapon and revelation — the flashing forth of a majesty too great to be wholly concealed, and the kindling of the coals that will fall as arrows on the enemy. So the dark pavilion of v.12 and the fiery brightness of this verse belong together, held in one breath: the same God is hidden and revealed, shelter to his servant and consuming fire to his foes, and the storm that terrifies the wicked is the very form his salvation takes for the man who cried to him out of the deep. What the enemy meets as judgment, the servant meets as rescue, and the single blaze of lightning is both at once.
14:
'The LORD thundered from heaven, and the most High uttered his voice.' Keil and Delitzsch read the thunder as the accompaniment of the lightning-arrows that follow: God sent his bolts upon the enemy amid violent thunder and thereby threw them into confusion. The verb they render 'discomfited' is, they note, the standing word for the destruction of a foe accomplished by God's own miraculous interposition — the same used of the confounding of Egypt at the sea (Exodus 14:24), of the panic before Joshua (Joshua 10:10), and of the rout at the thunder of 1 Samuel 7:10. The voice of the Most High is thus no mere sound in the sky but a weapon; when God speaks in the storm, armies melt.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott adds the textual comparison. Here the LORD thunders 'from heaven,' where the Psalm has 'in the heavens' — a difference lying in the original, and the Psalm further adds the words 'hail stones and coals of fire,' which this recension omits. Such variants, he holds, are the marks of two genuine but distinct recensions of one hymn, neither a careless copy of the other. The reformed John Gill refers the thunder to his exposition of the parallel psalm.

The witnesses do not divide over the sense, and the verse gains its force from its place in the theophany. The God who bowed the heavens (v.10) and rode the wind (v.11) now speaks, and his speech is thunder. Matthew Henry's caution at v.2 still governs the reading — David's battles were not literally won by thunderbolts — yet the figure insists on a truth of faith: the same power that terrified Israel's foes in the days of Samuel and Joshua was at work, unseen, for David. The thunder of heaven is the audible sign that the quarrel has passed out of human hands; when the Most High utters his voice, the outcome is already decided, and the enemy has only to be scattered. Faith, the witnesses imply, hears in every deliverance the far-off echo of that thunder, and knows that the God who spoke for David has not grown silent.
15:
'And he sent out arrows, and scattered them; lightning, and discomfited them.' The arrows, the witnesses agree, are the lightning of the storm turned to shafts against the enemy. Keil and Delitzsch keep the verse with the thunder of v.14 and the exposure of the deep in v.16: God sent lightning as arrows upon the enemies together with violent thunder, and threw them thereby into confusion — the verb again their standing term for a rout wrought by God himself. What in v.9 were coals kindled in his anger are here loosed as weapons, so that the fire of the divine displeasure becomes the artillery of heaven.

The reformed John Gill refers the arrows and lightning to his exposition of the twin psalm, reading them of the terror of God poured out on the foes of his servant. Matthew Henry, in his overview at v.2, names this among the figures of God's displeasure — his anger set forth by 'a smoke out of his nostrils, and fire out of his mouth, coals kindled, arrows' — and asks who can know the power and terror of such wrath, or stand before God when he is angry.

The verse admits no real debate; its interest lies in the completeness of the victory it pictures. The enemies are not merely repelled but 'scattered' and 'discomfited,' their ranks broken and their courage undone by a power they cannot see and cannot answer. David, drowning a moment before in the floods of v.5, lifts no weapon of his own; the arrows are God's, the lightning is God's, and the rout is God's. The lesson the witnesses draw together is that the deliverance of the afflicted and the confounding of their oppressors are one act of the same God — the fire that lights the servant's rescue is the same that scatters the foe, and both fall from the one open hand of heaven. That God fights so decisively for a hunted man is, on the reading Clarke and Henry press elsewhere, a foretaste of the greater victory in which every enemy of the true King is finally put to flight.
16:
'And the channels of the sea appeared, the foundations of the world were discovered, at the rebuking of the LORD, at the blast of the breath of his nostrils.' The storm reaches its uttermost, and the very floor of creation is laid bare. Keil and Delitzsch read it as the effect of the divine wrath in wind and earthquake, whereby the foundations of sea and land were exposed — the depth of the abyss and of the hell within the earth, into which the man to be rescued had fallen, uncovered by the breath of God, so that his hand might reach down and draw him out in v.17.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott notes the textual differences that cluster here. This text has 'channels of the sea' where the Psalm has 'of waters,' one of several slight variations that mark the two as distinct recensions; and the most striking change is that the closing pronoun shifts from 'his' here to 'thy' in the Psalm — the direct address being more appropriate to its use in public worship. The reformed John Gill refers the uncovering of the deep to his exposition of the parallel.

Matthew Henry's overview at v.2 gathers this verse with v.8 as the third thing the theophany sets forth — the extraordinary confusion into which David's enemies were thrown, 'as if the earth had trembled and the foundations of the world had been discovered.' The witnesses agree upon the double point. First, that no depth is hidden from God: the blast of his nostrils bares the sea-bed and the pillars of the world, and there is no abyss so deep that his rebuke cannot sound it. Second, that this cosmic upheaval serves one small mercy — the drawing out of a single sinking man. The whole frame of nature is shaken so that the arm of God may reach where no human help could go; and the terror that unmakes the world for the wicked is, for the servant, the clearing of the way to his rescue. What the enemy experiences as the collapse of every foundation, David experiences as the opening of a path down which the hand of God can come.
17:
Now the theophany bends to its purpose, and the storm becomes a rescue: 'He sent from above, he took me; he drew me out of many waters.' Keil and Delitzsch read it exactly against the preceding verse: the Lord stretched his hand from the height into the deep abysses that his wrath had uncovered, and drew out the man who was sinking there. The 'great waters,' they insist, do not stand for the enemy but for the peril itself — the floods of death and calamity in which David was going down; and the verb, used without the word 'hand,' pictures God reaching out and laying hold of him like a drowning man plucked from the sea.

The reformed John Gill refers the drawing-out to his exposition of the parallel, reading the many waters as the manifold troubles from which God delivered his servant. The image recalls the name of Moses, 'drawn out of the water,' and looks back to the breakers of death in v.5, now answered by a hand let down from heaven.

The witnesses are of one mind here, and the verse is the hinge on which the whole first half of the psalm turns. All the terror of vv.8–16 — the quaking earth, the smoking anger, the thunder and the arrows — was the approach of this one gesture: God bending down to take hold of a particular man. The greatness of the apparatus measures the greatness of the love; heaven is convulsed, the sea uncovered, and all so that a hand may reach the drowning David and draw him up. Matthew Henry's word at v.2 fits the moment — that God brings his people into deep difficulties 'that he may have the honour of saving them, and they the comfort of being saved by him.' The rescue is personal, direct, and undeserved, and it prepares for the note of covenant-delight that will sound in v.20. It is worth marking how the singer refuses every share in the credit: he did not swim, he did not climb, he was taken and drawn, passive in the grip of a stronger hand.
18:
'He delivered me from my strong enemy, and from them that hated me: for they were too strong for me.' Keil and Delitzsch take the verse with the rescue of v.17 and the enlargement of v.20, reading it as David's frank confession of his own weakness: the foe was mightier than he, and had God not intervened the outcome would have gone the other way. The candour is the point — the singer does not claim to have prevailed by his own arm but owns that he was overmatched, so that the deliverance is wholly God's.

Matthew Henry, in his overview at v.2, names the 'strong enemy' as Saul, 'who, if God had not succoured him, would have been too hard for him.' The whole reign of terror under Saul, and later the risings of Absalom and Sheba, are gathered into this admission: David was a man genuinely hunted and genuinely outnumbered, whose survival was no accident of skill but a rescue. The reformed John Gill refers the deliverance to his exposition of the parallel, reading the strong enemy of Saul and of every mighty adversary God subdued.

The witnesses do not divide, and the verse teaches by its very weakness. It is precisely because the enemy was 'too strong' that the glory belongs to God alone; a David who could have saved himself would owe no song. The confession here undergirds the whole theology of the psalm: God does not merely assist the strong but rescues the overmatched, so that the honour of the salvation returns entire to him. And in this David becomes, on the reading Henry and Clarke press elsewhere, a figure of a greater Sufferer, encompassed by enemies stronger than himself and delivered by the outstretched hand of the Father — the pattern of every rescue in which the weak are saved not by their power but by the power of the God who delights in them. The confession of being overmatched, far from shaming David, becomes the very thing that lets him give God all the glory. A man who wins by his own hand may keep the credit; a man who is rescued must give it away, and David, having been rescued, gives it away entire, naming not his sword but his God as the deliverer.
19:
'They prevented me in the day of my calamity: but the LORD was my stay.' The witness formally recorded here is the reformed John Gill, who refers the verse to his exposition of the parallel psalm; yet its two clauses set out the whole drama of the song in miniature. The enemies 'prevented' David — came upon him, that is, and forestalled him — in the very day when he was least able to bear it, choosing his hour of weakness for their assault; and against that timed and cruel onset stood one thing only, that 'the LORD was my stay.'

Matthew Henry's overview at v.2 gives the note the verse strikes: 'Am I burdened, and ready to sink? The Lord is my stay, by whom I am supported.' The word pictures a man on the point of collapse, held up not by his own footing but by another's grip. It answers to the many titles of vv.2–3 — rock, fortress, high tower — now proved in the day of calamity itself; the God confessed in the calm is the God who holds in the storm.

The verse has little exposition because it needs little; its force is in the contrast. On the one side, the day of calamity and the enemies who seize it; on the other, a single steadying hand. This is the pattern the whole psalm rehearses: the peril real and well-timed, the human resources spent, and the LORD interposing as the one support that does not give way. David does not say that the calamity was small or the foes few, but that in the midst of them he was upheld. The confession is meant for every afflicted saint after him — that the worst day, met with God as one's stay, is a day survived; and it opens directly into the enlargement of v.20, where the man held up in the narrow place is brought out into the wide. The verse thus stands as the pivot between danger and deliverance, its single adversative — 'but the LORD' — bearing the whole weight of the rescue.
20:
'He brought me forth also into a large place: he delivered me, because he delighted in me.' Though the reformed John Gill is the only witness formally entered here, referring the line to the parallel, this verse is one that Matthew Henry expounds with special warmth in his overview at v.2, and it crowns the whole account of David's rescue. The 'large place' is enlargement after confinement — David brought out of the strait where he was hemmed and hunted into room to thrive, his steps set at liberty, no longer straitened and confined.

But it is the last clause that Henry seizes: 'He delivered me, because he delighted in me.' The deliverance, he says, came not from common providence but from covenant-love; David was herein treated as a favourite, and perceived it in the communications of divine grace and comfort to his soul, and in the communion he had with God through the deliverance itself. And here Henry draws the typological line the chapter keeps offering: in this David was a type of Christ, 'whom God upheld because he delighted in him' (Isaiah 42:1). The rescued servant becomes the shadow of the beloved Son.

The witnesses, so far as they speak, do not divide; they let the verse carry the emotional weight of the first half of the psalm. Everything before it — the floods, the theophany, the hand from heaven — is here interpreted by a single word: delight. God did not save David because David was strong, or useful, or even because David cried, but because he delighted in him; the ground of the whole deliverance is love. This is the turn from what God did to why he did it, and it lifts the song from gratitude for rescue to wonder at being loved. The 'large place' is not merely safety but the openness of a life lived under favour — and it prepares for the searching self-examination of vv.21–25, where David asks in what sense such delight could rest on him. That a rescued sinner should trace his deliverance back past all secondary causes to the sheer good pleasure of God is, the witnesses agree, the deepest note the first half of the psalm can strike.
21:
With this verse the song turns from deliverance to its ground in David's integrity: 'The LORD rewarded me according to my righteousness: according to the cleanness of my hands hath he recompensed me.' The claim is startling on the lips of the man of Bathsheba, and the witnesses take care to define it. Keil and Delitzsch insist that the righteousness and cleanness David ascribes to himself are not a perfect holiness before God, but the righteousness of his aims and deeds as set against the wickedness of his persecutors; the verb, they note, means simply to deal with a man, for good or ill, as the Greeks said to do well or ill by someone. God dealt with David as his cause deserved, over against the false charges of his foes.

Matthew Henry, in his overview at v.2, guards the same point with a fine distinction: God recompensed David 'not for his righteousness, as if that had merited anything at the hand of God, yet according to his righteousness, which he was well pleased with, and had an eye to.' The reward is of grace, not of debt; God takes pleasure in the sincerity of his servant without making it the price of the mercy. David means chiefly his integrity toward Saul, Ishbosheth, Absalom, and Sheba, who accused him of being an ambitious and bloody man — a charge his conscience could deny.

The reformed John Gill refers the verse to his exposition of the parallel. The witnesses thus close ranks against two misreadings at once. On the one side, they refuse to let 'righteousness' become self-merit, as though David bought his rescue; on the other, they refuse to empty it of meaning, as if his integrity were nothing. The truth they hold together is that God, who saves by grace, is not indifferent to the character of those he saves: he vindicates the upright cause, answers the slandered conscience, and rewards, of his own good pleasure, a sincerity that he himself first wrought. The verse thus stands as a small but exact model of the gospel's own logic, in which grace both gives the righteousness and crowns it. The self-examination this opens will be spelled out in vv.22–25.
22:
'For I have kept the ways of the LORD, and have not wickedly departed from my God.' David unfolds the righteousness of v.21, and the witnesses read the two clauses as a positive and a negative — what he held to, and what he shunned. Keil and Delitzsch keep the verse within the strophe of self-vindication (vv.21–24), the language of a man measuring his life not by sinless perfection but by settled fidelity: he had kept God's ways as the tenor of his walk, and had not turned from him with the deliberate, high-handed apostasy that the phrase 'wickedly departed' implies.

Matthew Henry, in his overview at v.2, draws out just this qualification. David 'had not wickedly departed from his God': he could not say he had taken no false steps, but he had not deserted God nor forsaken his way; sins of infirmity he could not acquit himself from, but the grace of God had kept him from presumptuous sins. The distinction is the heart of the verse — between the stumbling of a true servant and the treachery of an apostate. The reformed John Gill refers the profession to his exposition of the parallel.

The witnesses agree, and their agreement rescues the verse from both pride and despair. It is not the boast of a man who never sinned; David wrote elsewhere the deepest of penitential psalms. It is the testimony of a man whose direction, through all his falls, was Godward — who kept the ways of the LORD as his road even when he tripped upon it, and never wickedly abandoned them. This is the integrity a saint may claim without presumption: not innocence, but constancy; not a spotless record, but an undivided allegiance. And it is precisely such fidelity, the witnesses hold, that God 'had an eye to' in v.21, rewarding of grace the sincerity he had himself planted and preserved. Understood so, the verse neither flatters David nor silences him; it lets a forgiven man speak honestly of a real, if imperfect, fidelity, and traces even that fidelity back to the God who kept him.
23:
'For all his judgments were before me: and as for his statutes, I did not depart from them.' David presses further the integrity of v.22, and the grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott notes a fine textual turn: this recension reads 'his statutes, I did not depart from them,' where the Psalm, by a very slight change in the original, has 'I did not put away his statutes from me.' The former is the more common form, he judges, while the latter suits better the parallelism there — one of the small, deliberate differences that distinguish the two texts.

Matthew Henry, in his overview at v.2, makes this the first branch of the witness of David's conscience: he 'had made the word of God his rule, and had kept to it.' Wherever David was, God's judgments were before him as his guide; whithersoever he went he took his religion along with him. Even when he was driven from his country and, as it were, sent to serve other gods, yet as for God's statutes he did not depart from them, but kept the way of the LORD and walked in it. The reformed John Gill refers the verse to his exposition of the parallel.

The witnesses do not contend, and together they define what David's claim to righteousness actually means. It is not an inward feeling but a fixed relation to the revealed will of God: the judgments kept 'before me' as a lamp for the road, the statutes never put away even under pressure of exile and danger. Henry's picture of a man carrying his religion with him into strange lands is the exact opposite of the fair-weather piety that keeps God's law at home and lays it aside abroad. David's integrity, on this reading, is measured by his constancy to Scripture in the hardest places — and it is this that grounds his confidence, in v.24, that he was upright before God. A piety that survives exile and danger, the witnesses imply, has been proved genuine as no comfortable devotion ever could be. The judgments kept before the eye in the wilderness are worth more than a hundred kept only within the safety of the sanctuary.
24:
'I was also upright before him, and have kept myself from mine iniquity.' The witness formally entered here is the reformed John Gill, referring the verse to the parallel; but the clause 'kept myself from mine iniquity' becomes the focus of the chapter's Messianic debate, raised by Adam Clarke and Kennicott at v.11. If the whole song is spoken by the Messiah, they concede, this phrase is an objection, for it seems to confess a personal sin; yet the difficulty is 'removed, in part, by the context, which represents the speaker as perfectly innocent and righteous,' and further by the ancient versions — the Syriac, Arabic, and Chaldee reading not 'mine iniquity' but 'iniquities,' a plural whose final letter, Kennicott holds, was improperly dropped by later transcribers. Read so, the line fits a sinless speaker who keeps himself from the sins of others.

Matthew Henry, taking David as the plain subject, reads the verse of a real and watchful holiness: David 'kept himself from his own iniquity,' not only in the famous instance of sparing Saul when he had him in his power, but in general, being 'afraid of sin and watchful against it,' and making conscience of what he said and did. Henry candidly enters the exception — 'the matter of Uriah' (1 Kings 15:5), a blot like that noted in Hezekiah's history (2 Chronicles 32:31) — so that David's uprightness is the sincere watchfulness of a saint, not the sinlessness of the Redeemer.

Here the two readings stand side by side and cannot be merged. For Henry and the grammatical-historical mind, David is the speaker, and 'mine iniquity' means his own besetting sin, from which grace kept him with one grievous lapse confessed. For Clarke and Kennicott, the verse itself, honestly weighed, tells against a Davidic reference and for the Messiah, provided the text be read as the versions read it. The witnesses thus divide not over the words only but over the whole question of the psalm: whether its 'I' is the flawed king who kept a general watch over his heart, or the sinless One in whom alone the claim to have kept clear of iniquity is simply true.
25:
'Therefore the LORD hath recompensed me according to my righteousness; according to my cleanness in his eye sight.' The verse rounds off the self-vindication by repeating the note struck in v.21, and the witnesses attend to its wording. Keil and Delitzsch observe the recension's peculiar forms — 'according to my righteousness' spelled as in v.21, and an expression for 'cleanness' that is, they say, without example elsewhere, where the usual idiom is 'cleanness of hands.' The variations are the kind of dialectal or scribal peculiarity that marks this text off from the smoother Psalm.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott notes the same simplification: 'to my cleanness' stands here for the Psalm's more poetical 'to the cleanness of my hands.' The reformed John Gill refers the verse to his exposition of the parallel. (The note printed here under Jamieson, Fausset and Brown belongs, by an evident misplacement, to the book of Ruth, and has no bearing on David's song.)

The repetition is not idle, and the witnesses let it stand as a deliberate frame. What vv.22–24 asserted — fidelity to God's ways, constancy to his statutes, watchfulness over sin — is here gathered up and sealed with the word 'therefore,' as the ground on which the LORD recompensed him. Yet the phrase 'in his eye sight' keeps the claim humble: the cleanness is such as God sees and approves, not such as David boasts before men, and it is measured by the divine eye that reads the heart. Taken with Henry's caution at v.21 — reward 'according to' but not 'for' his righteousness — the verse holds grace and integrity in their proper order: God first works the cleanness, then owns it, then rewards it, so that even the recompense of the upright is, at bottom, a gift. From this settled confidence the psalm turns outward, in vv.26–28, to the general rule of God's dealings with all men. What David has found true in his own case he now sees as the fixed manner of God with every soul, and the repeated 'therefore' binds his private mercy to that universal law.
26:
The song now turns from David's own case to a general law of God's dealings: 'With the merciful thou wilt show thyself merciful, and with the upright man thou wilt show thyself upright.' Keil and Delitzsch open the strophe (vv.26–28) here, noting its distinctive reflexive verb-forms — God 'showing himself' merciful, upright, pure — and its variants from the Psalm; the thought is that God answers men in kind, meeting each according to the character he brings. This is not caprice but correspondence: the divine response mirrors the human disposition.

Matthew Henry, in his overview at v.2, states the rule that governs the whole strophe: 'As we are found towards God, he will be found towards us.' God's mercy and grace will be the joy of those that are merciful and gracious — for even the merciful need mercy, and they shall obtain it; his uprightness, his justice and faithfulness, will be the joy of the upright and faithful, both toward God and man. The reformed John Gill refers the verse to his exposition of the parallel.

The witnesses agree, and the principle they draw out is one of the great commonplaces of Scripture, that God deals with men as they deal with him and with one another. Yet, rightly understood, it is no doctrine of merit. The mercy that God rewards he first bestowed; the man is merciful because grace made him so, and God's meeting him with mercy is the crown of his own prior gift. What the verse excludes is the fantasy that a hard, false, and cruel man might yet find God soft toward him; the God of David is not mocked, and reflects back upon each the moral image he has chosen. For the merciful this is unspeakable comfort; for the froward, as v.27 will make plain, it is a warning — that the God who answers in kind will answer even the crooked in their own coin. The same law that comforts the gracious is a terror to the hard, for both alike will find God to be toward them what they have been toward him.
27:
The rule of correspondence set out in v.26 is carried to its sharp edge: 'With the pure thou wilt show thyself pure; and with the froward thou wilt show thyself unsavoury.' Charles Ellicott corrects the last word: 'unsavoury' should rather be 'froward,' for though the Hebrew form here is anomalous it is the same word, and stands to the previous term just as in the Psalm — God showing himself perverse, as it were, to the perverse. Keil and Delitzsch, weighing the recension's peculiar verb-forms, note the boldness of the expression and observe that a word implying folly or absurdity cannot properly be ascribed to God, so that the form must be read as a reflexive: with the crooked, God shows himself one who baffles and outwits their crookedness.

Matthew Henry, in his overview at v.2, keeps the two halves of the law together. God's purity and holiness will be the joy of those that are pure and holy, who therefore give thanks at the remembrance of it; but those who turn aside to crooked ways he will 'lead forth with the workers of iniquity.' With the froward he will wrestle, and those with whom God wrestles are sure to be foiled: 'Woe unto him that strives with his Maker!' The reformed John Gill refers the verse to his exposition of the parallel.

The witnesses agree, and they guard the verse against a slander it easily invites — that it makes God himself crooked. It does no such thing. The froward meet in God not a matching perversity but a holy resistance perfectly fitted to defeat them; his 'frowardness' toward them is simply his purity turned, of necessity, against their impurity. To the clean he is transparently clean, and they rejoice in it; to the twisted he is an adversary who turns their own cunning against them, so that their schemes recoil upon their heads. The same God is thus experienced as sweetness or as gall according to what men bring to him, and the difference lies wholly in them. It is a solemn word to set beside the comfort of v.26: the God who cannot be deceived cannot be used, and every crooked approach to him is met and undone.
28:
The strophe of God's dealings closes with a double stroke of mercy and judgment: 'And the afflicted people thou wilt save: but thine eyes are upon the haughty, that thou mayest bring them down.' Keil and Delitzsch mark this as the hinge into the second half of the psalm: the explanatory clause that opens v.29 looks back to the thought here, that David himself belonged to the 'afflicted people' whom the Lord always helps. The verse states the principle of which David's whole life is the illustration — God stoops to save the lowly and stiffens against the proud.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott notes the wording: this text says 'thine eyes are upon the haughty,' where the Psalm has the briefer and more common 'wilt bring down high looks.' The reformed John Gill refers the verse to his exposition of the parallel. Matthew Henry, in his overview at v.2, gathers the pastoral point: if any of God's good people be afflicted people, he will save them, 'either out of their afflictions or by and after them'; while as for the haughty, his eyes are upon them, marking them out to be brought down, 'for he resists the proud.'

The witnesses do not divide, and they let the verse crown the section with a single antithesis: the afflicted saved, the haughty humbled. Two things are worth marking with them. First, that God's saving eye and his abasing eye are the same eye; the very attentiveness that is rescue to the lowly is doom to the proud. Second, that David places himself deliberately among the afflicted, not the mighty — the king who could look back on great victories will not claim to have risen by his own height, but confesses that he was saved as one of the lowly people whom God lifts. The pride that God brings down is precisely the temper David refuses; and so the general law of vv.26–28 becomes the doorway into the second half of the song, where the God who saves the afflicted arms his lowly servant for victory over the nations. The order is instructive: only when David has confessed himself among the afflicted and disowned the temper of the haughty does the psalm let him sing of conquest, so that his triumphs come as the reward of humility, not its contradiction.
29:
The second half of the song opens on a note of light: 'For thou art my lamp, O LORD: and the LORD will lighten my darkness.' Keil and Delitzsch mark this verse as the beginning of the description of the help David had received, and still would receive, in his conflict with the enemies of Israel. They note a textual roughness — a word for 'lightening' seems to have fallen out, and the recension calls the LORD a 'lamp' where elsewhere he is named 'light' (as in Psalm 27:1) — but the sense is plain: God himself is the light by which his servant sees his way, and by whom his darkness is turned to brightness.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott traces the figure. This text has 'thou art my lamp,' where the Psalm changes the image to 'thou wilt light my candle,' and he gathers the kindred passages in which the lamp is a token of a continued house and kingdom (Psalm 132:17; 1 Kings 11:36; 1 Kings 15:4). The reformed John Gill refers the lamp to his exposition of the parallel. Matthew Henry, in his overview at v.2, phrases the comfort as a question and answer: 'Am I in the dark, benighted, at a loss? Thou art my lamp, O Lord! to show me my way, and thou wilt dispel my darkness.'

The witnesses do not contend, and the verse rewards their attention as the turn of the whole psalm. The first half sang of rescue from death; the second sings of light for the road ahead, and both are God himself — not merely a light he gives, but the LORD who is the lamp. Henry's benighted traveller is every believer who cannot see the next step; the answer is not a torch put into his hand but the presence of God as his light. And Ellicott's cluster of 'lamp' passages quietly reintroduces the note that will close the song: the lamp that never goes out in David's house is the pledge of the enduring kingdom, and so of the Seed in whom, as v.51 will declare, that lamp burns for ever.
30:
'For by thee I have run through a troop: by my God have I leaped over a wall.' The verse celebrates a strength for war that David traces wholly to God, and Keil and Delitzsch read it so: in the strength of his God he could run against hostile troops and leap their walls — that is, overcome every hostile power. They pause on the verb, defending 'run' (from a root meaning to run, not one meaning to smash) and noting that it may be construed with the accusative in the sense of rushing upon a troop; the parallelism with 'leap over a wall' favours the picture of a warrior charging and scaling. The reformed John Gill refers the verse to his exposition of the parallel psalm.

Matthew Henry's overview at v.2 supplies the theology the terse line assumes. David, bred to the shepherd's crook, was yet well instructed in the arts of war and qualified for its toils and perils; for God, having called him to fight his battles, fitted him for the service, giving him courage, skill, and strength. The running and leaping of this verse are the outworking of that equipping — feats beyond the man's native power, done 'by thee' and 'by my God.'

The witnesses agree, and the verse teaches by the little words it repeats. Twice David names the source: 'by thee,' 'by my God.' The exploits are real — troops broken through, walls surmounted — but the strength is borrowed, and David is careful to say so at the very moment of boasting. It is the same humility that owned him overmatched in v.18, now carried into the hour of triumph: the God who rescued the sinking man is the same who nerves the fighting one, so that neither the deliverance nor the victory leaves David anything to claim. The whole of his prowess, like the whole of his safety, hangs on the two words 'by thee' — and the second half of the psalm will spell out, victory by victory, what that dependence achieved. There is no boasting in the flesh here, only a boasting in the Lord, and the reader is taught that the boldest feats of the life of faith are still, from first to last, the works of God in his servant.
31:
David lifts his eyes from his own exploits to the God who gives them, and the verse becomes a small creed: 'As for God, his way is perfect; the word of the LORD is tried: he is a buckler to all them that trust in him.' Keil and Delitzsch observe that David grounds his confidence on two things — the acts of God and the word of God. The divine title is written absolutely, 'the God,' as in the Song of Moses (Deuteronomy 32:4); his way is perfect, without blemish, and his word is refined brass, pure silver (compare Psalm 12:6); and he who trusts him is safe from every foe. They note that the last two clauses recur among Agur's proverbs (Proverbs 30:5), a saying evidently prized and passed on.

Matthew Henry, in his overview at v.2, draws the same two supports into a contrast with human faithlessness: 'Men begin in kindness, but end not; promise, but perform not; but God will finish his work, and his word is tried, and what we may trust.' God's way is perfect where man's is broken off half-done. The reformed John Gill refers the verse to his exposition of the parallel. (The comment printed here under Jamieson, Fausset and Brown belongs, by an evident misplacement, to the story of Ruth and does not touch David's song.)

The witnesses do not divide, and together they root the whole second half of the psalm in the character of God rather than the skill of David. Three things are affirmed and held out to every believer. God's way is perfect — his providence, however dark, is without flaw. His word is tried — tested like refined metal and found true, so that every promise will stand. And he is a buckler, a shield, not to David only but 'to all them that trust in him,' so that the confidence sung here is offered to any who will take God at his word. The verse thus universalises David's experience: what proved true for the hunted king is pledged to all the trusting, and the perfection of God's way is the surest ground a soul can stand on.
32:
'For who is God, save the LORD? and who is a rock, save our God?' The rhetorical questions expect the answer none, and the witnesses take them as the theological pillar under David's confidence. Keil and Delitzsch put it plainly: there is no true God who can help, beside or beyond Jehovah, and they hear behind the words the same challenge sounded in the Song of Moses (Deuteronomy 32:31) and by Hannah (1 Samuel 2:2). The word 'rock' is the same great title with which the psalm opened in v.2, so that the song circles back to its beginning and rests again on the incomparable God.

Matthew Henry, in his overview at v.2, makes this the first act of David's worship: 'Who is God, save the LORD? All others that are adored as deities are counterfeits and pretenders. None is to be relied on but he. Who is a rock, save our God? They are dead, but the Lord liveth' (v.47) — the idols disappointing their worshippers in the hour of need, while the living God alone stands firm. The reformed John Gill refers the verse to his exposition of the parallel.

The witnesses agree, and the verse guards David's whole song from being misheard as self-congratulation. The victories of the second half are not the achievements of a great man among lesser men, but the works of the only God among counterfeit gods. By asking 'who is a rock, save our God?' David denies to every rival — every idol of the nations he will shortly conquer — any share in strength or deliverance. There is one Rock; all others are dead. And the little word 'our' is not to be missed: the incomparable God is not a distant absolute but the covenant God of David and of Israel, the God who is confessed as belonging to his people even as they belong to him. On that exclusive and personal foundation the triumphs of vv.33–46 are built. To ask 'who is God, save the LORD?' in the hour of victory is to hand the whole triumph back to its Author before it can swell the victor's head.
33:
'God is my strength and power: and he maketh my way perfect.' The grammatical-historical commentators are drawn at once to a variant. Charles Ellicott prefers 'my strong fortress' to 'my strength and power,' and notes that the Psalm carries a quite different thought at this point — 'It is God that girdeth me with strength' — a thought this recension holds back until v.40. Keil and Delitzsch render the line 'This God is my strong fortress, and leads the innocent his way,' defending the reading against a later correction that made it 'my way'; God is the guide who leads the upright along the road he is to walk.

The reformed John Gill refers the verse to his exposition of the parallel. Matthew Henry, in his overview at v.2, reads the perfecting of the way as God's crowning of David's undertakings: 'God made his way perfect, gave him success in all his undertakings' — so that the clause looks back to the perfect way of God confessed in v.31 and forward to the perfect way God makes for his servant.

The witnesses do not seriously divide; even the textual differences point one way. Whether one reads 'strength and power' or 'strong fortress,' the confession is that David's whole competence for life and war is God — not a gift God once gave and then withdrew, but God himself as the singer's strength. And the second clause is the sweeter for standing beside v.31: the God whose own way is perfect is the God who 'maketh my way perfect,' so that the flawless providence of the Almighty is bent to order and complete the path of one frail man. David does not claim that his road was smooth or short — it ran through wilderness and rebellion — but that God perfected it, bringing it, for all its turns, to its appointed end. What God is in himself, he becomes for those who trust him; the perfection that is his by nature he lends to their pilgrimage by grace, and the crooked road of a hunted life is straightened, in the end, by the hand that made it.
34:
'He maketh my feet like hinds' feet: and setteth me upon my high places.' The single voice formally entered is the reformed John Gill, who refers the verse to his exposition of the parallel; yet the image is vivid and the commentators' overview gives it a full sense. The hind — the swift mountain deer — is the figure of a swiftness and sure-footedness given for war, and the 'high places' are the heights of safety and command to which God lifts his servant.

Matthew Henry, in his overview at v.2, draws out both halves. Among the successes God crowned David with was that he was made 'very expeditious: He maketh my feet swift like hinds' feet,' a swiftness 'of great advantage both in charging and retreating' — the soldier who can strike quickly and, when need be, withdraw untaken. And God 'set him upon his high places, denoting both safety and dignity' — the deer's rocky heights standing for the security and the honour to which David was raised above his enemies. Keil and Delitzsch, keeping the verse with vv.33–35, render it 'He makes my feet like the hinds, and setteth me upon my high places,' as part of the equipment for victory that God bestows.

The witnesses agree, and the verse is worth pausing over for the delicacy of its praise. David does not merely thank God for winning his battles; he thanks him for the very qualities of body and skill by which they were won — the quick foot, the sure step on dangerous ground. Nothing in his competence is treated as his own endowment; the agility that saved his life in a hundred skirmishes is received as a gift, 'he maketh.' And the ascent to 'high places' is likewise God's doing, not David's climbing: he is set there, lifted to safety and dignity by the same hand that made his feet swift. It is the picture of a life in which even natural gifts are grace, and every height is a place to which one has been carried. The deer's sure foot on the crag and the throne on the hill of Zion are, in David's reckoning, one gift from one Giver, and neither owed anything to the man who received them.
35:
'He teacheth my hands to war; so that a bow of steel is broken by mine arms.' Again the reformed John Gill is the single witness formally entered, referring the verse to the parallel; and again Matthew Henry's overview at v.2 unfolds it. Among the gifts by which God fitted David for the battles he called him to, Henry names skill above mere force: God 'made him very ingenious — He teacheth my hands to war — and this ingenuity was as good as strength, for it follows, so that a bow of steel is broken by my arms, not so much by main force as by dexterity.' The bending of the great bow is the token not of a giant's muscle but of a trained and God-taught hand.

Keil and Delitzsch, keeping the verse within the strophe of equipment (vv.33–35), render it 'He teacheth my hands to fight, and my arms span brazen bows,' reading it of the martial competence God imparts. The point on which all agree is the little word 'teacheth': David's very warcraft is instruction from above.

The witnesses do not divide, and the verse completes the portrait begun in v.34. There the gift was speed; here it is skill; and both are traced to God. It is a striking thing that a warrior should credit his God not merely with victory but with his very technique — the eye, the aim, the strength of arm that bends the toughest bow. David will not divide his life into a sacred part that God gives and a secular part that he manages himself; his skill in arms is as much a divine endowment as his deliverance from death. Henry's word that 'ingenuity was as good as strength' carries a quiet lesson: God equips his servants with the particular abilities their calling demands, and the humblest and the highest of these — a shepherd's aim turned to a soldier's — are alike his teaching. The hands that break the bow of steel were trained in the same school as the heart that kept his statutes.
36:
'Thou hast also given me the shield of thy salvation: and thy gentleness hath made me great.' The verse turns from David's equipment to the tenderness behind it, and the witnesses fasten on the word rendered 'gentleness.' Keil and Delitzsch take the recension's term to mean not God's 'humiliation' but his 'hearkening' — that practical answering of God when he is called upon for help, shown in his making David's steps broad and clearing the stumbling-blocks from his way. Charles Ellicott agrees that the word, differing very slightly from the Psalm's 'thy gentleness,' means by its etymology 'thy answering,' God's response to the prayers offered; and he notes that the Psalm inserts here the line, absent from this text, 'and thy right hand hath holden me up.'

The reformed John Gill refers the verse to his exposition of the parallel. Matthew Henry, in his overview at v.2, keeps the word 'gentleness' and reads it richly: among the successes God gave him, 'God's gentleness, his grace and tender mercy, made him great,' gave him great wealth and authority and a name like that of the great men of the earth. The greatness of David is thus traced not to his prowess but to God's kindness.

The witnesses divide only on a fine point of translation, and the two readings prove complementary. Whether the word be 'gentleness' or 'answering,' the sense is that David's rise came by God's condescending kindness — the shield that saves being 'the shield of thy salvation,' and the greatness that follows being the fruit of divine tenderness, not human ambition. There is a deliberate paradox in the line that the witnesses feel without labouring: it is God's gentleness that makes a man great. The world makes men great by hardness, force, and self-assertion; David confesses that he was lifted by the very softness of God toward him — the stooping, hearkening kindness that answered his cry and made his footing broad. Greatness so gotten is safe to carry, for it never forgets whose gift it is; and the God who condescends to answer a hunted man's prayer is the same who exalts him, so that the height and the humility are held together in one grace.
37:
'Thou hast enlarged my steps under me; so that my feet did not slip.' Keil and Delitzsch render it 'Thou makest my steps broad under me, and my ankles have not trembled,' and read it as the continuation of the divine hearkening of v.36: God gives the walker a broad space for free motion, removing obstructions from his path, so that he moves without stumbling. The reformed John Gill refers the verse to his exposition of the parallel psalm.

Matthew Henry, in his overview at v.2, gathers this with the enlargement of v.20 as the liberty God gave his servant: his steps 'were enlarged under him, so that he had room to stir, being no longer straitened and confined.' The picture is of a man who had walked a long while in narrow and dangerous places — hunted through wildernesses, cornered in caves — now given a wide, firm road on which the foot can plant itself securely.

The witnesses agree, and the verse is a quiet parable of the whole deliverance. Two mercies are named, and they belong together: room and stability. God 'enlarged' the steps — gave breadth, freedom, scope where before there had been constriction; and because the ground was broad and level under him, the feet 'did not slip.' The narrow ledge invites the fall; the broad path steadies the walk. David traces both to God: not only the space but the security, not only the room to move but the footing that does not betray him. It answers to the 'large place' of v.20, and it foreshadows the settled kingdom in which David would at last walk at liberty. For the believer who reads it after him, the promise is the same — that the God who makes the way perfect (v.33) also holds the foot upon it, so that enlargement and safety are given by one hand. To be given room without being given steadiness would be a doubtful mercy; David has both together, and traces them alike to the one God who neither cramps his people in their going nor suffers them to fall.
38:
The song passes to the victories themselves: 'I have pursued mine enemies, and destroyed them; and turned not again until I had consumed them.' Keil and Delitzsch raise a point of grammar with theological weight. The verbs, they argue, are not merely past but reach into the future: David refers not only to the victories he has already won but to those he will yet win, so that the whole description is at once record and prophecy of a dominion still expanding. They note too that this recension's wording obliterates an echo of the Song of the Sea (Exodus 15) that the Psalm preserves — the language of Egypt's overthrow lying just beneath David's triumph-song.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott adds that where this text has 'destroyed them,' the Psalm has 'overtaken them,' the milder word meant to suggest the same thing as the plainer one here. The reformed John Gill refers the verse to his exposition of the parallel. Matthew Henry, in his overview at v.2, reads the line as the note of thoroughness: David's were 'speedy victories — I turned not again till I had consumed them — and complete victories.'

The witnesses do not divide, and the verse has a hardness that repays honest reading. This is the language of ancient holy war, in which the enemies of God's anointed and of his people are pursued to their destruction; and the commentators do not soften it into metaphor. Yet two things frame it. First, the tenses that Keil and Delitzsch mark lift the verse above mere bloodshed into the promise of a kingdom that will subdue all opposition — a note the New Testament will take up in the reign of David's greater Son, who must reign till every enemy is put under his feet. Second, the Exodus echo makes David's victory a fresh act of the God who drowned Pharaoh: the same LORD who broke Egypt breaks David's foes. The thoroughness Henry praises is thus not David's cruelty but God's completeness — the refusal of the divine deliverance to leave its work half done.
39:
'And I have consumed them, and wounded them, that they could not arise: yea, they are fallen under my feet.' The verse presses the totality of the conquest, and the witnesses attend to its force. Keil and Delitzsch, continuing the strophe (vv.38–41), render it 'I will consume them and dash them in pieces, that they may not arise, and may fall under my feet,' again keeping the future edge: the enemies are so broken that they cannot rise again. Charles Ellicott notes the textual heightening — the clause 'I have consumed them' is wanting in the Psalm, and the word for 'wounded' there needs a stronger rendering, 'crushed them.' The reformed John Gill refers the verse to his exposition of the parallel.

Matthew Henry, in his overview at v.2, gathers the language of these verses into one heap of finality: the enemies of Israel were 'wounded, destroyed, consumed, fell under his feet, trampled upon, and disabled to rise, and their necks lay at his mercy.' The picture is of a defeat from which there is no recovery.

The witnesses do not divide, and the verse belongs to the same grave key as v.38. Its point is not the singer's ferocity but the completeness of God's victory through him — enemies not merely repelled but so crushed that they 'could not arise.'
