The phrase 'fallen under my feet' is the language later Scripture will lift to a higher pitch, when the enemies of the true King, and death itself, are put under his feet. Read in the light of the chapter's Messianic thread, David's total triumph over the foes of God's people becomes a shadow of that final subjugation in which no enemy is left standing. The witnesses hold the historical and the typical together: a real, thorough victory in David's own wars, and in it a promise of the day when every power that rises against the Lord's Anointed shall be brought down and rise no more. What is grievous in the letter becomes, in the light of that promise, the pledge of a peace secured by the utter overthrow of all that would destroy it.
40:
'For thou hast girded me with strength to battle: them that rose up against me hast thou subdued under me.' The single voice formally entered is the reformed John Gill, referring the verse to his exposition of the parallel; yet the line is one Keil and Delitzsch keep within the victory-strophe (vv.38–41), rendering it 'Thou girdest me with strength for war, Thou bowest mine adversaries under me,' and continuing to read the verbs with a forward reach — a strength given not for one campaign only but for a dominion still to come. It is worth noting, as Ellicott observed at v.33, that this is the point where the Psalm places the thought 'God girdeth me with strength' which the recension held back from its earlier verse.

Matthew Henry, in his overview at v.2, reads the verse as David handing back the credit for his very valour: 'Thou hast girded me with strength to battle; he gives God the glory of all his courage and ability for service.' The girding is God's act; the subduing is God's act; David wears his strength as a belt buckled on him by another.

The witnesses agree, and the verse keeps the second half of the psalm from ever tipping into self-praise. David is a mighty man of war, and he says so; but every clause returns the might to God. The two verbs — 'thou hast girded,' 'thou hast subdued' — divide the whole business of war between them, the arming and the outcome, and leave David nothing but to be the instrument. The strength that clothes him for battle is put on him like armour from God's own hand, and the enemies who 'rose up against' him are bowed not by his prowess but by God's subduing. So even at the height of his conquests the singer confesses himself a man equipped and used, and the glory of the victory runs back, undivided, to the God who girded him. It is the settled posture of the whole song, and the mark of a heart that has learned where its true strength lies: to fight with all one's might, and yet to know that the might itself was lent.
41:
'Thou hast also given me the necks of mine enemies, that I might destroy them that hate me.' The idiom of 'giving the necks' pictures the foe in flight, the back of the neck turned as they run, or bowed to the conqueror's foot — either way, a routed and helpless enemy handed over. Keil and Delitzsch render it 'Thou makest mine enemies turn the back to me; my haters, I root them out,' keeping the note struck since v.38, that the victory is thorough and God-given. The reformed John Gill refers the verse to his exposition of the parallel psalm.

Matthew Henry, in his overview at v.2, gathers this among the tokens of complete conquest: the enemies of Israel were consumed and disabled to rise, 'and their necks lay at his mercy.' The phrase marks the moment when resistance is over and the outcome is no longer in doubt — the enemy's neck under the victor's heel.

The witnesses do not divide, and the verse repays a careful reading of its grammar of grace. Once again the decisive verb is God's: 'Thou hast given me the necks of mine enemies.' David does not say that he took them, but that they were given; the rout is a gift, laid in his hand by the God who fought for him. His own part — 'that I might destroy them that hate me' — is real, but it is the exercise of a victory already granted, not the winning of one still in doubt. The pattern is the same that has run through the whole song: God supplies the deliverance, God supplies the strength, God supplies even the routed enemy, and David acts within the space that God's prior grace has opened. Read with the chapter's Messianic thread, the enemies given under the anointed king's feet foreshadow the day when every foe of the true King is made his footstool, and the last hater of God's people is put down for ever. The victory David could win only field by field, his greater Son will win once and for all, and the necks that were given to the shepherd-king are a pledge of that final subjection.
42:
'They looked, but there was none to save; even unto the LORD, but he answered them not.' The verse turns to the despair of the beaten foe, and the witnesses read it with a certain gravity. Keil and Delitzsch take the recension's verb to mean 'they look out' or 'look up expectantly' — the enemies casting about for a deliverer, even lifting their eyes to their gods, and finding none. Charles Ellicott notes the textual crux: by the change of a single letter this 'they looked' becomes in the Psalm 'they cried,' so rendered by the Septuagint here as well, one of the readings being plainly a clerical slip. The reformed John Gill refers the verse to his exposition of the parallel.

Matthew Henry, in his overview at v.2, presses the reason the cry went unanswered: David's enemies 'cried both to earth and heaven for help, but in vain. There was none to save, none that durst appear for them.' God did not answer them — for they were not on his side, nor did they cry to him until they were driven to the last extremity; a repentance forced only by ruin is no repentance at all.

The witnesses agree, and the verse holds up a sober mirror to v.7. There David, in his distress, called upon the LORD and was heard 'out of his temple.' Here his enemies, in their distress, look and even cry — and heaven is silent. The difference is not that God is arbitrary but that David called on the God he trusted and served, while his foes turned to him only when every other hope had failed, and turned as enemies still. Henry's point is the searching one: a cry wrung out by extremity, from a heart that never sought God until it was cornered, is not the prayer of faith. The same God who cannot be deceived (v.27) will not be used as a last resort by those who despised him while they prospered. The verse is thus no arbitrary silence of heaven but the just answer to a lifelong refusal, and it stands as a warning to every reader not to leave the seeking of God until the day of calamity makes it too late.
43:
'Then did I beat them as small as the dust of the earth, I did stamp them as the mire of the street, and did spread them abroad.' The verse pictures a defeat ground to powder, and the witnesses attend closely to its imagery. Keil and Delitzsch render it 'I rub in pieces as the dust of the earth, like the mire of the streets I crush them and stamp upon them,' and judge that the two figures — dust and mire — here appear in a somewhat 'distorted' form beside the Psalm's, the recension having weakened the vivid 'dust before the wind' into the plainer 'dust of the earth.' Charles Ellicott agrees that the Psalm combines in one compact figure — 'dust before the wind' — what is here spread out into two clauses, and holds the change to be designed, heightening the poetic effect. The reformed John Gill refers the verse to his exposition of the parallel. (The note here ascribed to Jamieson, Fausset and Brown belongs, by an evident misplacement, to the book of Ruth and has no bearing on the song.)

Matthew Henry, in his overview at v.2, catches the two images together: David 'beat them as small as the dust of the earth, which is scattered by the wind and trodden on by every foot.' Dust to be blown away, mire to be trampled — the enemy reduced to what is lightest and what is lowest.

The witnesses do not divide over the sense, only over which recension paints it better, and the verse completes the account of David's conquests on the same grave note as v.39. The language is deliberately extreme — powder and street-mud — to mark a victory beyond recovery. Yet the reader is not to lose the thread that has held through all these verses: it is God who girded, God who subdued, God who gave the necks; David's grinding of the foe to dust is the outworking of a triumph granted from heaven. The completeness that sounds merciless is, in the theology of the psalm, the completeness of God's own deliverance, which does not leave the enemies of his people half-standing to rise and threaten again.
44:
The song lifts from private warfare to empire: 'Thou also hast delivered me from the strivings of my people, thou hast kept me to be head of the heathen: a people which I knew not shall serve me.' Keil and Delitzsch read the 'strivings of my people' precisely of David's domestic conflicts — the opposition of Ishbosheth and the rebellions of Absalom and Sheba — out of which the Lord delivered him; and these very deliverances, they observe, formed the prelude and basis of his dominion over the heathen, so that the line 'Thou preservest me to be the head of the nations' falls exactly into place. Rescue at home was the road to rule abroad. The reformed John Gill refers the verse to his exposition of the parallel.

Matthew Henry, in his overview at v.2, reads David's signal preservations as the sign that he was 'designed and reserved for something great — to rule over all Israel, notwithstanding the strivings of the people,' and to be head of the heathen, so that those whom he had not known should serve him. Henry hears in this the note that carries the song beyond David: as the Son of David 'goes on conquering and to conquer' (Revelation 6:2), so a people once strangers is brought to serve the King.

The witnesses agree, and the verse marks the widening of the whole psalm's horizon. David's kingdom, born out of civil strife and secured against domestic rebellion, reaches out to nations he never knew, who submit to his rule. The grammatical-historical commentators keep this firmly in David's own reign; but the reach of the language — a people 'which I knew not' brought to serve — is what feeds the chapter's Messianic reading, and Paul's use of a later verse of this song for the conversion of the Gentiles (Romans 15:9) confirms the trajectory. What David tasted in the submission of Moab and Edom, his greater Son would fulfil in the gathering of the nations; and the deliverance from the strivings of his own people becomes the first step toward a dominion without borders.
45:
'Strangers shall submit themselves unto me: as soon as they hear, they shall be obedient unto me.' The verse describes the reach of David's dominion over foreign peoples, and the witnesses weigh both its grammar and its quality. Keil and Delitzsch note the recension's reflexive verb — the sons of the stranger 'made themselves flatterers,' feigning a submission — and render the second clause 'upon hearsay they obey me,' the mere report of David's power bringing distant peoples to their knees. Charles Ellicott observes that this clause and the one before it are transposed in the Psalm, a difference of arrangement rather than of sense. The reformed John Gill refers the verse to his exposition of the parallel.

Matthew Henry, in his overview at v.2, catches the note of feigned obedience that the Hebrew implies: the sons of the stranger, hearing the report of David's victories and the tokens of God's presence with him, 'would be possessed with a fear of him, would be forced to submit to him, though feignedly, and would be obedient to him.' The submission is real in fact but hollow in heart — the yielding of the overawed rather than the loyal.

The witnesses do not divide, and the verse is honest about the limits of conquest. David's empire could compel obedience 'as soon as they hear'; the very rumour of his strength bent the necks of nations. But the commentators, following the Hebrew, will not pretend that outward submission is the same as love. The strangers 'submit themselves,' yet the word carries a flavour of dissembling; they obey, yet feignedly, out of fear. This is the most that earthly dominion can win — a compelled and cringing loyalty — and it quietly points beyond itself. The kingdom of David's greater Son will not rest in a submission wrung from dread, but will seek the willing hearts of the nations; and the difference between the feigned obedience of the overawed and the glad service of the redeemed is the difference between the shadow here and the substance to come. David's empire could bend the knee of the nations; only the gospel of his greater Son can win their hearts, and the honest note of feigned obedience in this verse leaves room for that better conquest.
46:
'Strangers shall fade away, and they shall be afraid out of their close places.' The witnesses here are the reformed John Gill, referring the verse to his exposition of the parallel, and the grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott, who lingers on a textual nicety. The English follows the Psalm, but the Hebrew verbs differ by the transposition of a letter; probably a mere clerical error, yet if the reading here be retained the sense shifts a little. The Psalm means 'came trembling from their fastnesses,' picturing the conquered submitting in fear; this text suggests 'came limping from their fastnesses,' hinting that the remnant of the enemy had already been injured and wounded. Keil and Delitzsch render the recension 'The sons of the stranger despair, and tremble out of their castles.'

The picture, on any reading, is of a broken and fearful enemy. The strongholds that once made them bold — the 'close places,' the mountain fastnesses and walled towns — now become the scene of their dread; they 'fade away,' their courage wilting like a leaf, and creep out of their defences either trembling or limping, undone before they are even reached.

The witnesses do not divide over the substance, and the verse rounds off the account of David's conquests with a telling image. The very fortresses in which the nations trusted are turned to prisons of fear; there is no close place close enough to shut out the terror of the God who fights for David. It answers, from the far side, to the confidence of v.3, where God himself was David's high tower and refuge: the strongholds of the ungodly cannot save, while the LORD who is his people's stronghold cannot fail. And the fading, limping remnant here sets in relief the living, exalted God of the closing strophe — for as the strangers wilt out of their castles, David turns, in v.47, to bless the Rock who lives and reigns. The last thing the psalm shows us of the enemy is not their strength but their fear, and the last thing it will show us of God is not his terror but his enduring mercy.
47:
The concluding strophe of praise begins: 'The LORD liveth; and blessed be my rock; and exalted be the God of the rock of my salvation.' Keil and Delitzsch insist on the exact force of the opening formula: it does not mean 'let the LORD live,' as a wish, but is a declaration — 'the LORD is living' — and as such is itself an act of praise. To bless God, they note with Hengstenberg, is simply to ascribe to him the glorious perfections that are already his: 'we have only to give him what is his own.' The clauses that follow are likewise declarations of what God is — living, blessed, exalted — rather than petitions for him to become so. The reformed John Gill refers the verse to his exposition of the parallel.

Matthew Henry's overview at v.2 supplies the contrast the verse turns on. Over against the idols, which are dead and disappoint their worshippers in the hour of need, David exults that his God is the living one — 'the God of the rock of my salvation,' from whom and in whom his saving strength is found. The verse gathers up all the titles of vv.2–3 and lifts them into doxology.

The witnesses agree, and the verse marks the great turn from narrative to worship. David has told his story — the floods, the theophany, the rescue, the conquests — and now he does not merely thank God for what he did but blesses God for what he is. The point Keil and Delitzsch press is worth holding: praise adds nothing to God; it only renders him his due, confessing the perfections he possesses whether men own them or not. And the heart of the confession is that the LORD 'liveth.' Against the dead gods of the routed nations of v.46, and against death itself out of whose snares he was drawn, David sets the living God — the Rock who was his salvation and who, being alive for ever, will be the salvation of his seed after him. To say 'the LORD liveth' at the end of a life so full of deadly perils is the strongest of all confessions: the God who outlives every danger will outlive the singer too, and keep his covenant when David is in the grave.
48:
'It is God that avengeth me, and that bringeth down the people under me.' The witnesses read the verse as part of the closing doxology (vv.47–49), in which David names the God he has just blessed by the things he does. Keil and Delitzsch render it 'The God who giveth me vengeance, and bringeth nations under me,' noting that the recension's word for subduing is less pointed and less elegant than the Psalm's, but the sense is one: the vengeance David took on his enemies was not his private revenge but God's righteous act through him, and the subjection of the nations was God's doing. The reformed John Gill refers the verse to his exposition of the parallel.

The 'vengeance' here is not the malice of a wronged man but the just recompense of God upon those who had risen against his anointed and his people. David does not claim to have avenged himself; he confesses that God avenged him — a distinction the whole psalm has laboured to keep. The bringing down of the nations belongs to the same hand that girded him for war in v.40 and gave him the necks of his enemies in v.41.

The witnesses do not divide, and the verse guards David's triumph from becoming a boast of personal vindictiveness. It is 'God that avengeth me': the initiative, the right, and the power all belong to him. This is the temper the New Testament will commend to the believer — 'Vengeance is mine, I will repay, saith the Lord' — and it is already David's here, who leaves the reckoning with his enemies in God's hands rather than taking it into his own. The subduing of the peoples under him is, in the same breath, denied to his own strength and ascribed to God. So the closing strophe keeps faith with the whole song: even in the hour of settled dominion, David's confession is not 'my hand hath done this' but 'it is God' — the living God of v.47 who both saves and repays. A king who could so easily have credited his own sword instead names his God as the avenger, and in that naming keeps his throne from becoming an idol.
49:
'And that bringeth me forth from mine enemies: thou also hast lifted me up on high above them that rose up against me: thou hast delivered me from the violent man.' The formal witness is again the reformed John Gill, referring the verse to the parallel; and Keil and Delitzsch, closing the strophe (vv.47–49), render it 'Who leadeth me out from mine enemies, and exalteth me above mine adversaries, delivereth me from the man of violence' — noting that the recension's phrasing here is somewhat peculiar and unsupported elsewhere, and that its plural 'men of violence' is exchanged for a singular, 'the violent man.'

Matthew Henry, in his overview at v.2, reads the exaltation as the crown of David's whole history: he was 'lifted up on high, as high as the throne, above those that rose up against him.' The 'violent man' from whom he was delivered is, in the first place, Saul — the malicious and powerful adversary named at the very head of the song in v.1 — and every like enemy after him.

The witnesses agree, and the verse draws the account of deliverance to its close by binding the end to the beginning. The song opened with David delivered 'out of the hand of all his enemies, and out of the hand of Saul' (v.1); it closes here with the same confession — brought forth from his enemies, lifted above his adversaries, delivered from the violent man. The three verbs mark three mercies: rescue out of the enemies' reach, exaltation above them, and deliverance from the worst of them. And each is God's: 'thou hast lifted me up,' 'thou hast delivered me.' David ends where he began, not on a throne he seized but on a height to which he was raised, and the last word about his foes is not his victory over them but God's deliverance of him from them — which frees his mouth for the thanksgiving of v.50. A man delivered has something to sing; a man who merely conquered has only something to boast, and David, knowing which he is, turns from the record of his enemies to the praise of his God.
50:
'Therefore I will give thanks unto thee, O LORD, among the heathen, and I will sing praises unto thy name.' The verse gathers the whole song into a resolve of public praise, and the witnesses find in it a reach beyond David. Keil and Delitzsch observe that the grace shown to David was so great that its praise could not be confined within the narrow bounds of Israel: with the spread of David's dominion over the nations went also the knowledge, and so the praise, of the LORD who gave him the victory. And they mark the New Testament seal on this — that Paul quotes this very verse (Romans 15:9), together with the Song of Moses (Deuteronomy 32:43) and a psalm of the nations (Psalm 117:1), to prove that the Gentiles were always meant to glorify God for his mercy. The reformed John Gill refers the verse to his exposition of the parallel.

Matthew Henry, in his overview at v.2, reads the resolve as David's fearless public confession: 'To him will I give thanks, and that publicly. When he was among the heathen he would neither be afraid nor ashamed to own his obligations to the God of Israel.' The praise is not private gratitude but open testimony, sung where the nations can hear.

Here the chapter's Messianic thread and its plain history meet without strain. David really did give thanks to the LORD among the heathen he had subdued; and Paul, reading the verse as Scripture, hears in it the far-off purpose of God to gather the Gentiles into the praise of his mercy through great David's greater Son. The witnesses agree that the two senses do not compete: the historical David, singing God's praises among conquered nations, is the very type of the gospel's advance, in which the name once confessed only in Israel is now sung among all peoples. What began as one man's thanksgiving for rescue becomes, in the apostle's hands, the charter of a worldwide worship — the fulfilment toward which, as v.51 will show, the whole song has been moving.
51:
The song closes on the covenant: 'He is the tower of salvation for his king: and sheweth mercy to his anointed, unto David, and to his seed for evermore.' The witnesses gather here both a textual question and the theological summit of the whole psalm. Charles Ellicott notes that 'He is the tower of salvation' follows the Hebrew margin, while the text — supported by the ancient versions and by Psalm 18:50 — reads 'Great deliverance giveth he,' the difference between the two words being extremely slight. He draws from this brief review of the two recensions a broad lesson: Providence has dealt with the manuscripts of the Old Testament as with those of the New, securing them through the long succession of ages from all substantial error, yet not so overruling human action but that mere slips of the pen should sometimes creep in. Keil and Delitzsch, weighing the whole comparison, conclude against Hengstenberg that this recension is not a superior or designedly Davidic revision of the Psalm; with few exceptions there is little in the text of 2 Samuel that commends itself above the other. The reformed John Gill refers the verse to his exposition of the parallel.

But the witnesses do not end in textual criticism; they end in the promise. Ellicott reads the whole psalm as 'a grand anthem of thanksgiving of David... and of his well-assured hope in the fulfilment of the Divine promise of the perpetuity of his kingdom through the coming of Him in whom all the families of the earth should be blessed' (Genesis 12:3). Matthew Henry, in his overview at v.2, presses the same point: the mercy shown to David's anointed reaches 'to that seed of his for evermore,' resting on the promise God made never to take his mercy from David's house (2 Samuel 7:15); and on that promise David depends 'with an eye to Christ, who alone is his seed for evermore, whose throne and kingdom still continue,' whereas David's natural line is long since extinct (compare Psalm 89:28).

So the chapter's long debate over David and the Messiah is resolved not by choosing between them but by joining them in the covenant. Whether one reads the song as of David only (the grammatical-historical view), or of the Messiah only (Clarke and Kennicott), or of David as the type of his greater Son (Henry), the last word gathers all into 'his seed for evermore.' The historical David is real, his deliverances real, his kingdom real; but his mercy runs on past his grave to the Anointed in whom the promised throne endures for ever. Henry's closing word is the fitting seal on the whole: 'Thus all his joys and all his hopes terminate, as ours should, in the great Redeemer.' The tower of salvation that sheltered the king shelters his seed; and the song that began in one man's rescue ends in the everlasting mercy of God to David and to great David's greater Son.
